questions.question_text: How many hours of post-licensing education must a newly licensed Indiana broker complete, and within what timeframe?
questions.correct_answer: 30 hours within the first 2 years of licensure
distractors: 24 hours within the first year of licensure
36 hours within the first 3-year renewal cycle
30 hours before the first license renewal
questions.explanation: Indiana requires new brokers to complete 30 hours of post-licensing education within their first two years of licensure. This is separate from the ongoing CE requirement. Failing to complete post-licensing education on time can prevent renewal of the broker's license.

questions.question_text: A broker receives her Indiana license in January 2024. When must she complete her post-licensing education?
questions.correct_answer: By January 2026
distractors: By January 2027 at her first renewal
By December 2024 within the first calendar year
By January 2025 within the first year
questions.explanation: The post-licensing requirement of 30 hours must be completed within 2 years of the license issue date. A license issued in January 2024 creates a post-licensing deadline of January 2026, regardless of when the first renewal cycle falls.

questions.question_text: A new Indiana broker completes only 20 of the required 30 post-licensing hours by the end of her second year. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: She cannot renew her license until post-licensing education is complete
distractors: She receives a 6-month grace period to finish the remaining hours
She may renew at reduced active status until the hours are complete
Her license automatically converts to inactive status for one year
questions.explanation: Post-licensing education is a prerequisite for license renewal in Indiana. Failing to complete the full 30 hours means the broker cannot renew until the requirement is satisfied. There is no grace period or reduced-status option described in Indiana law for this specific deficiency. Failure to complete postlicensing education also subjects the broker to action under IC 25-1-4. Because the 30 hours substitute for CE during the first two years, the broker cannot attest to education compliance at renewal until the hours are done.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker went inactive before finishing the required 30 hours of post-licensing education. What must she do before her license can be reactivated?
questions.correct_answer: Complete the full 30 hours of post-licensing education before reactivation
distractors: Complete only the 12 CE hours for the current year
Retake the state licensing examination
Nothing — post-licensing obligations end when a license goes inactive
questions.explanation: Under IC 25-34.1-3-10(f), a broker who becomes inactive before completing the 30-hour post-licensing requirement must complete those hours before the license can be reactivated. The same rule applies to referral status (IC 25-34.1-3-10.5). No exam retake is required.

questions.question_text: A newly licensed Indiana broker is also an experienced CPA who advises real estate investors on tax matters. Does his CPA credential reduce his post-licensing education obligation?
questions.correct_answer: No, the post-licensing requirement applies regardless of other professional credentials
distractors: Yes, licensed professionals may waive up to 15 hours of post-licensing education
Yes, if the CPA's real estate tax work overlaps with post-licensing topics
Only IREC may grant a waiver based on equivalent professional experience
questions.explanation: Indiana's post-licensing education requirement applies to all new brokers and does not provide waivers based on other professional licenses or experience. The 30-hour requirement is specific to real estate practice education and must be completed through IREC-approved providers.

questions.question_text: How many continuing education hours must an Indiana broker complete each year?
questions.correct_answer: 12 hours per year
distractors: 6 hours per year
36 hours per year
24 hours per year
questions.explanation: Indiana requires 12 hours of continuing education per year, totaling 36 hours over the 3-year renewal cycle. The 36-hour figure applies to the full cycle, not a single year. The 12-hour annual requirement ensures brokers stay current on an ongoing basis.

questions.question_text: A managing broker completes her 12 hours of CE for the year but none of those hours were in management skills courses. Is she in compliance?
questions.correct_answer: No, managing brokers must complete 4 of 12 annual CE hours in management skills
distractors: Yes, the 4-hour management requirement only applies at license renewal
Yes, any 12 hours of approved CE satisfies all requirements
No, managing brokers must complete all 12 hours in management topics
questions.explanation: Indiana requires managing brokers to include at least 4 of their 12 annual CE hours in management skills topics. The remaining 8 hours may be any approved real estate CE. Completing general CE without the 4-hour management component does not satisfy the managing broker requirement.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker completes 36 hours of CE in year one of his 3-year renewal cycle. Can he skip CE in years two and three?
questions.correct_answer: No, 12 hours are required every year, not just over the 3-year cycle
distractors: Yes, front-loading CE is permitted as long as the 3-year total is met
Yes, but only if he notifies IREC of the front-loaded completion
No, but he may carry over a maximum of 6 hours to each subsequent year
questions.explanation: Indiana's CE requirement is 12 hours per year — not simply 36 hours over 3 years. Completing extra hours in one year does not allow skipping CE in subsequent years. There is no carryover provision for excess annual hours.

questions.question_text: A broker's CE provider offers a 4-hour course titled 'Advanced Negotiation for Real Estate Professionals.' A managing broker takes this course. How does it count toward her requirements?
questions.correct_answer: As general CE hours only — not toward the 4-hour management skills requirement
distractors: As all 4 required management skills hours for the year
As 2 general hours and 2 management hours at the broker's election
It cannot be counted until IREC individually approves the course for managing brokers
questions.explanation: To count toward the 4-hour management skills requirement, a course must be specifically approved as covering management topics as defined by IREC. Negotiation, while useful, is a general professional skill — not necessarily a management skills course. The managing broker must verify the course's approved category before relying on it to satisfy the management hours requirement.

questions.question_text: Over a full 3-year renewal cycle, how many total CE hours must an Indiana managing broker complete, including the management skills component?
questions.correct_answer: 36 total hours, including at least 12 in management skills
distractors: 36 total hours with no specific topic breakdown
48 total hours with 12 in management skills
36 total hours with at least 4 in management skills
questions.explanation: Managing brokers complete 12 CE hours per year = 36 hours over the 3-year cycle. Of the 12 annual hours, 4 must be in management skills, meaning at least 12 of the 36 cycle hours are in management topics. The requirement is 4 per year, not 4 per cycle.

questions.question_text: How long is an Indiana real estate broker's license renewal cycle?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years
distractors: 1 year
2 years
4 years
questions.explanation: Indiana broker licenses are renewed on a 3-year cycle. This aligns with the CE requirement of 12 hours/year × 3 years = 36 total hours per renewal period. Annual renewal is not required.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker is licensed but not currently affiliated with a managing broker. What status best describes her license?
questions.correct_answer: Unassigned status
distractors: Inactive status
Referral status
Suspended status
questions.explanation: An Indiana broker who holds a current license but is not affiliated with a broker company is in unassigned status. Inactive status means the broker has affirmatively chosen not to practice. Referral status is a separate category allowing limited referral activity. Unassigned and inactive are distinct classifications.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to keep her license current but does not plan to practice real estate. Which status allows her to maintain the license without active practice obligations?
questions.correct_answer: Inactive status
distractors: Unassigned status
Referral status
Suspended status
questions.explanation: Inactive status allows a broker to maintain a valid license without engaging in brokerage activities. It is the appropriate choice for someone who wants to preserve future re-activation options. Unassigned means no current broker-company affiliation — the broker cannot practice and, unlike an inactive broker, must still complete CE. Referral status allows limited referral activity only.

questions.question_text: A broker on referral status in Indiana receives a request from a former client to show properties and write a contract. Can the broker do this?
questions.correct_answer: No, referral status limits activity to referring clients to active brokers
distractors: Yes, referral status allows all licensed activities if unpaid
Yes, but only for former clients with whom the broker has a prior relationship
No, the broker must first upgrade to unassigned status before showing property
questions.explanation: Referral status restricts the broker to making referrals only — showing property and writing contracts are full brokerage activities that require active affiliated status. The broker would need to change to active affiliated status before engaging in these activities. The restriction applies regardless of whether compensation is involved.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker on inactive status wants to reactivate. Which of the following conditions must typically be met?
questions.correct_answer: Complete the CE required for the current licensing period and affiliate with a broker company to resume practice
distractors: Retake the state licensing examination and reapply for licensure
Complete 90 hours of pre-license education and pass the exam again
Obtain IREC approval at a formal hearing before reactivating the license
questions.explanation: IC 25-34.1-3-10 requires an inactive broker to fulfill the CE requirements for the current licensing period (or any outstanding post-licensing hours) before applying for reactivation; back-years of CE are not owed. On reactivation the broker elects to be assigned to a broker company or unassigned; assignment is required to actually practice. No exam retake, repeat pre-license education, or hearing is required.

questions.question_text: An Indiana broker decides to change from one managing broker to another. Who must be notified?
questions.correct_answer: The Indiana Real Estate Commission
distractors: Only the old managing broker
Only the new managing broker
Both managing brokers but not IREC
questions.explanation: When a broker changes firms, the Commission must be notified. The affiliation of a broker with a managing broker is a matter of public record with IREC. While professional courtesy suggests notifying the former and new managing brokers, the legal obligation runs to the Commission.

questions.question_text: A managing broker in Indiana terminates a broker's affiliation with the firm. What obligation does the managing broker have?
questions.correct_answer: Notify the Commission of the termination
distractors: Hold the broker's license for 30 days before releasing it
File a complaint with IREC if the broker left voluntarily
Provide the broker a written release before IREC is notified
questions.explanation: Indiana law requires the managing broker to notify the Commission when a broker's affiliation is terminated, regardless of the reason for the departure. The managing broker does not hold the license — it belongs to the individual broker. A written release is not a legal prerequisite to IREC notification.

questions.question_text: A managing broker unexpectedly closes her firm due to a family emergency. Two affiliated brokers are now without a managing broker. What is the immediate concern regarding their license status?
questions.correct_answer: The brokers become unassigned and cannot practice until affiliated with a new managing broker
distractors: The brokers automatically transfer to the nearest IREC-approved firm
The brokers may continue practicing independently for up to 90 days
The brokers' licenses are suspended automatically until they find a new firm
questions.explanation: Without an affiliated managing broker, brokers fall into unassigned status and may not engage in brokerage activities. There is no automatic transfer to another firm, and independent practice is not permitted for brokers (as opposed to managing brokers). The license itself is not suspended — the brokers simply cannot actively practice until affiliation is re-established.

questions.question_text: A broker changes his legal name after marriage. Which of the following correctly describes his obligation under Indiana license law?
questions.correct_answer: Notify IREC of the name change and update license records
distractors: Apply for a new license under the new name within 30 days
Only update his name at the next scheduled renewal
No action is required unless the name change affects his firm's name
questions.explanation: Licensees must keep their personal information current with IREC, including legal name changes. This is a notification and update obligation, not a new license application. Waiting until renewal would leave official records inaccurate and could create complications with transaction documents and legal proceedings.

questions.question_text: A broker affiliated with a managing broker's firm becomes a managing broker herself and opens her own company. What steps must she take regarding her license?
questions.correct_answer: Upgrade to managing broker, obtain a broker company license for the new entity, and notify IREC
distractors: Apply for a branch office permit under her current managing broker's license
Transfer her existing broker license to managing broker status with no additional steps
Notify only her clients of the firm change; IREC notification is optional
questions.explanation: Opening a new brokerage requires that she: (1) qualify as a managing broker (3 years of active licensure, a 24-hour management course, and the managing broker exam under IC 25-34.1-4-0.5), (2) obtain a broker company license for the new entity with herself designated as managing broker, and (3) notify the Commission of the association change (IC 25-34.1-3-6; 876 IAC 8-1-4). A broker cannot simply transfer a license or operate a separate entity under another managing broker's license.
